my_thoughts: The timing/definition works: when ready (not immediately), redefines: will = desire (negative/positive, affects others), distinguishes: good vs. bad will (desire against/for work). The student stated commitment (testing teacher commitment), which shows they understand: prepared to obey = should get directions (transaction). They'll likely accept the timing/definition. The episode is complete: contradiction → resolution → guidance → condition/warning → commitment → timing/definition. Pattern complete.
accumulated_insight: Three-turn pattern: T1: Student stuck on logical impossibility (stuck on logical impossibility)—resolution: will = strong desire, functional protocol. T2: Student requested instruction (seeking external guidance)—condition/warning: personal direction = high-stakes, obedience required. T3: Student stated commitment ('If one is prepared to obey, will you give directions?')—testing teacher commitment via states commitment and requests transaction. They understand: prepared to obey = should get directions (transaction). The comparison_contrast mechanism sets timing: when ready (not immediately), redefines: will = desire (negative/positive, affects others), distinguishes: good vs. bad will (desire against/for work). Pattern complete: contradiction question → resolution → guidance question → condition/warning → commitment question → timing/definition. They've shifted: directions = when ready (not immediately), will = desire (good/bad) (timing/definition).
visible_response:
If opportunity offers.
It must be at a moment when you have will.
There must be a definite desire to do something that affects work or other
people.
Usually we have bad will;
we very seldom have good will.
If you
have good will, I do not speak about it;
I simply say, 'Go on, continue,
learn'.
